title: EXPOSÉ XVI GROUPS OF NULL UNIPOTENT RANK

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XVI GROUPS OF NULL UNIPOTENT RANK

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XVI
GROUPS OF NULL UNIPOTENT RANKby M. Raynaud01

footnotes.html: Cf. note on page 1 of Exp. XV.

blocks.html: 1. An immersion criterion
1.1. Examples of monomorphisms of group preschemes which are not immersions.

We are going to construct a prescheme S, two S-group preschemes G and H, and an S-group monomorphism \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) which is not an immersion. The groups G and H will be of finite presentation over S, flat over S, and G will even be smooth over S (cf. Exp. VIII, paragraph and note (∗) preceding 7.1, see also XVII App. III, 4).

Let us first take for S the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, of unequal characteristic and of residue characteristic equal to 2. Let \(t\) be the generic point of S and \(s\) the closed point.

a) Take for G the open subgroup of the constant group \(\mathrm{G}' = (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}\) obtained by removing the point of the closed fibre \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_s\) distinct from the identity element, and take for H the group of multiplicative type \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)_{\mathrm{S}}\) of \(2^{\text{nd}}\) roots of unity (Exp. I 4.4.4). In view of the choice of S, \(\mathrm{H}_t\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_t\) while \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is a radicial group. We have an obvious morphism \(\mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{H}\), defined by the section \((-1)\) of H, whence a morphism \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\), which is a monomorphism, but which is not an immersion (otherwise it would necessarily be an isomorphism (cf. Exp. VIII 7)). Here, Exp. VIII 7.9 does not apply, because \({}_2\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}\) is not finite over S.

b) Denote by K the S-group prescheme, étale and non-separated, obtained from the unit group by "doubling" the unique point of the closed fibre. Take for H the product group: \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_2)_{\mathrm{S}} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{K}\). Let \(a\) (resp. \(b\)) be the unique section of \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_2\) (resp. K), over S, which is distinct from the unit section, and let \(h = (a, b)\) be the corresponding section of H. The datum of \(h\) defines an S-group morphism: \[ u : \mathrm{G} = (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}. \] It is clear that \(u\) is a monomorphism and it is not an immersion. Here, Exp. VIII 7.9 does not apply, because \({}_2\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{H}\) is not separated over S.
c) More interesting is the following example, where G is a smooth group with connected fibres (and even \(\mathrm{G} = (\mathbb{G}_a)_{\mathrm{S}}\)) (cf. Exp. VIII 7.10).
Take for S the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A of equal characteristic 2 and let \(\pi\) be a uniformizer of A and \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal of A.

Consider the additive group \((\mathbb{G}_a)_{\mathrm{S}}\), the product group \((\mathbb{G}_a \times \mathbb{G}_a)_{\mathrm{S}}\) with ring \(\mathrm{A}[\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}]\), and let \(\mathrm{G}_1\) be the closed subgroup with equation: \[ (1) \qquad \mathrm{X}^2 + \mathrm{X} - \pi\mathrm{Y} = 0. \]

The group \(\mathrm{G}_1\) is smooth over S, its generic fibre is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{G}_a)_t\) and its closed fibre is isomorphic to the product of \((\mathbb{G}_a)_s\) by \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_s\). For the factor \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_s\) one can take the subgroup \(\mathrm{N}_s\) of \((\mathrm{G}_1)_s\) with equation: \[ (2) \qquad \mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{X}^2 + \mathrm{X} = 0. \]

Let \(\mathrm{N}'\) and \(\mathrm{N}''\) be two group subschemes of \(\mathrm{G}_1\), isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}\), whose closed fibres coincide with \(\mathrm{N}_s\) and whose generic fibres are distinct. The groups \(\mathrm{N}'\) and \(\mathrm{N}''\) are thus defined by the datum of two sections of \(\mathrm{G}_1\) over S with coordinates \((a', b')\) (resp. \((a'', b'')\)) such that: \[ (3) \qquad \left\{ \begin{aligned} & a'^2 + a' - \pi b' = a''^2 + a'' - \pi b'' = 0, \\ & a' \text{ and } a'' \equiv 1 \pmod{\mathfrak{m}}, \quad a' \neq a'' \\ & b' \text{ and } b'' \equiv 0 \pmod{\mathfrak{m}}. \end{aligned} \right. \] (One can take for example \((1, 0)\) and \((1 + \pi^2, \pi + \pi^3)\).)

The quotient groups \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}_1/\mathrm{N}'\) and \(\mathrm{G}'' = \mathrm{G}_1/\mathrm{N}''\) are representable (Exp. V 4.1). Let \(u\) be the canonical morphism: \[ u : \mathrm{G}_1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}' \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{G}'', \] and let H be the group subscheme of \(\mathrm{G}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}''\) equal to the schematic closure in \(\mathrm{G}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}''\) of \(u_t(\mathrm{G}_1)_t\), so that \(u\) factors through H. The kernel of \(u\) is \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{N}' \cap \mathrm{N}''\), whose generic fibre is the unit group and whose closed fibre is equal to \(\mathrm{N}_s\); in particular, K is not flat over S. On the generic fibre, \(u_t\) is therefore an isomorphism of \((\mathrm{G}_1)_t\) onto \(\mathrm{H}_t\). On the other hand, I claim that \(\mathrm{H}_s\) is not smooth. Indeed, if \(\mathrm{H}_s\) were smooth, then since \(u_s\) is a morphism with finite kernel and \((\mathrm{G}_1)_s\) and \(\mathrm{H}_s\) have the same dimension (namely 1), \(u_s\) would be a flat morphism; since \(\mathrm{G}_1\) and H are flat, \(u\) would be a flat morphism and consequently \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) would be flat over S, which is not the case. It is clear that the restriction of \(u\) to the identity component G of \(\mathrm{G}_1\) is a monomorphism (the fibres of \(\mathrm{K} \cap \mathrm{G}\) are unit groups, hence \(\mathrm{K} \cap \mathrm{G}\) is the unit group (Exp. VIB 2.9)), but it is not an immersion (otherwise it would be an open immersion and \(\mathrm{H}_s\) would be smooth). Here, Exp. VIII 7.9 does not apply, because \({}_2\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}\) is not finite over S.

For lovers of equations, let us say that in the above example one can take for H the closed subgroup of \((\mathbb{G}_a \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathbb{G}_a)\) whose ring is \(\mathrm{A}[\mathrm{V}, \mathrm{W}]/(\mathrm{F})\) with: \[ \mathrm{F} = (a''b' - b''a')(a''^2\mathrm{V} - a'^2\mathrm{W}) - (a''\mathrm{V} - a'\mathrm{W})^2 \] (where \(a', a'', b', b''\) satisfy \((3)\)). For G, one takes the group \((\mathbb{G}_a)_{\mathrm{S}}\) with ring \(\mathrm{A}[\mathrm{T}]\), and for the morphism \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\), the S-morphism defined by the maps: \[ \mathrm{V} \longmapsto a'(a'\mathrm{T} + \pi\mathrm{T}^2), \qquad \mathrm{W} \longmapsto a''(a''\mathrm{T} + \pi\mathrm{T}^2). \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.2

The preceding construction is inspired by the method of Koizumi-Shimura2. It does not apply when S is the spectrum of a ring of unequal characteristic, the points of finite order of G being then "too rigid".
1.3. Statement of the immersion criterion.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.3

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth over S, of finite type, possessing Cartan subgroups locally for the fpqc topology (Exp. XV 6.1 and 7.3 (i)), H an S-group prescheme locally of finite type, and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-group morphism. Then:

a) If G has connected fibres, in order for \(u\) to be an immersion, it is (necessary and) sufficient that for every S-scheme \(\mathrm{S}'\) which is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, and for every Cartan subgroup C of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), the restriction of \(u_{\mathrm{S}'}\) to C be an immersion.

b) In order for \(u\) to be an immersion (resp. a closed immersion), it is (necessary and) sufficient that for every \(\mathrm{S}'\) as above and every Cartan subgroup C of the identity component \((\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'})^0\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), the restriction of \(u_{\mathrm{S}'}\) to the normalizer (Exp. XI 6.11) N of C in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) be an immersion (resp. a closed immersion).
Before proving 1.3, let us state some applications:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth over S, of finite presentation over S, of null unipotent rank (Exp. XV 6.1 ter) and possessing, locally for the fpqc topology, maximal tori, H an S-group prescheme, and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-group monomorphism. Suppose moreover that either H is of finite presentation over S, or S is locally noetherian and H locally of finite type. Then:

a) If G has connected fibres, \(u\) is an immersion.

b) If H is separated over S and if for every \(\mathrm{S}'\) over S and every maximal torus T of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), the Weyl group \[ \mathrm{W} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T})/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}^0}(\mathrm{T}) \] is representable (a condition always fulfilled if G has connected fibres (Exp. XV 7.1 (iv))) and is finite over S, then \(u\) is a closed immersion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth over S, of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres, H an S-group prescheme, and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-group monomorphism. Suppose that either H is of finite presentation over S, or S is locally noetherian and H locally of finite type. Then:

a) If G is reductive, i.e. is affine over S with reductive fibres (cf. Exp. XIX), \(u\) is an immersion, and a closed immersion if H is separated.

b) If G has affine, solvable, connected fibres of null unipotent rank, \(u\) is an immersion, and a closed immersion if H is separated.
Proof of 1.4 from 1.3.

Reduction to the case S noetherian. If S is locally noetherian, the reduction is immediate, the properties to be proved being local on S. In the second case G and H are of finite presentation over S. After restricting S, we may assume S affine. By Exp. VIB §10, there exist a noetherian scheme \(\mathrm{S}_0\), an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-group prescheme \(\mathrm{G}_0\), smooth over \(\mathrm{S}_0\) (with connected fibres in case a)), of finite type over \(\mathrm{S}_0\), an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-group prescheme \(\mathrm{H}_0\) of finite type (separated in case b)), and an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-group morphism \[ u_0 : \mathrm{G}_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_0, \] such that G, H, \(u\) are obtained from \(\mathrm{G}_0\), \(\mathrm{H}_0\), \(u_0\) by the base extension \(\mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{S}_0\). The fact that \(u\) is a monomorphism translates as \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u\) = unit group; we may therefore assume that \(u_0\) is a monomorphism. Since G is of null unipotent rank \(\rho_u\) and possesses maximal tori locally for the fpqc topology, the abelian rank \(\rho_{ab}\) of the fibres of G is locally constant (Exp. XV 8.18). But \(\rho_u\) and \(\rho_{ab}\) are locally constructible functions (Exp. XV 6.3 bis). A standard argument (cf. EGA IV 8.3.4) shows that one can choose \(\mathrm{S}_0\) and \(\mathrm{G}_0\) in such a way that the unipotent rank (resp. the abelian rank) of the fibres of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is null (resp. locally constant). But then \(\mathrm{G}_0\) possesses maximal tori locally for the fpqc topology (Exp. XV 8.18) and the functor \(\mathscr{TM}_{\mathrm{G}_0}\) of maximal tori of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is representable by an \(\mathrm{S}_0\)-scheme \(\mathrm{X}_0\), of finite type over \(\mathrm{S}_0\) (Exp. XV 8.15). Let \(\mathrm{T}_0\) be the "universal" maximal torus of \((\mathrm{G}_0)_{\mathrm{X}_0}\), X the S-prescheme \(\mathrm{X}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathrm{S}\), and \(\mathrm{T} = \mathrm{T}_0 \times_{\mathrm{S}_0} \mathrm{S}\) the universal maximal torus for G. By hypothesis, in case b), the Weyl group relative to T is representable and finite over X. These two properties are compatible with projective limits of preschemes (Exp. VIB 10.1 iii) and EGA IV 8.10.5). One can therefore choose \(\mathrm{S}_0\) so that the Weyl group of \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is finite over \(\mathrm{X}_0\). It is clear that under these conditions, to prove 1.4, we may replace S, G, \(u\), H by \(\mathrm{S}_0\), \(\mathrm{G}_0\), \(u_0\), \(\mathrm{H}_0\), hence assume S noetherian.

Let us use the valuative criterion provided by 1.3. We are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring and to the case where \(\mathrm{G}^0\) possesses a Cartan subgroup C.

Proof of 1.4 a). We must show that the restriction of \(u\) to C is an immersion (1.3 a)), which reduces us to the case where \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{C}\) has connected nilpotent fibres. Standard reductions (cf. Exp. VIII proof of 7.1) reduce us to the case where H is flat over S, \(u_t\) is an isomorphism on the generic fibre and \(u_s\) is an isomorphism of the underlying spaces on the closed fibre. The group H then has connected fibres, hence is separated over S (Exp. VIB 5.2). Let us grant for a moment the lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme, smooth over S, with connected, nilpotent fibres, of null unipotent rank. Then:

i) G is commutative.

ii) For every integer \(n > 0\), \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is a group prescheme, flat and quasi-finite over S, finite over S if and only if the abelian rank, or the reductive rank, of G is locally constant on S.

iii) For every integer \(q > 0\) invertible on S, the family of subgroups \({}_{q^n}\mathrm{G}\) is universally schematically dense in G relative to S (EGA IV 11.10.8).

Lemma 1.6 applies to the group G, and since G possesses maximal tori locally, the reductive rank of the fibres of G is locally constant, hence (1.6 ii)), for every integer \(n > 0\), \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is finite over S. The fact that \(u\) is an immersion then follows from Exp. VIII 7.9.
Proof of 1.6. Let us first examine the case where S is the spectrum of a field:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7

Let \(k\) be a field of characteristic \(p\) and G a smooth, connected, nilpotent \(k\)-algebraic group of null unipotent rank. Then G is a commutative group, an extension of an abelian variety A by a torus T. For every integer \(n > 0\), \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is a finite group, étale if \((n, p) = 1\), defined by a \(k\)-algebra of rank \(n^{\rho_r + 2\rho_{ab}}\). If \((q, p) = 1\), the family of subgroups \({}_{q^n}\mathrm{G}\) \((n \in \mathbb{N})\) is schematically dense in G.

Proof of 1.7. Let Z be the centre of G. The group \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is affine (Exp. XII 6.1), of null unipotent rank, smooth and connected, hence is a torus (BIBLE 4 th. 4), but then G is commutative (Exp. XII 6.4). If T is the unique maximal torus of G (Exp. XV 3.4), it follows immediately from Chevalley's theorem (Sém. Bourbaki 1956/57, N°145) that G is an extension of an abelian variety A by T. For every integer \(n > 0\), raising to the \(n\)-th power is an epimorphism in T, whence an exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow {}_n\mathrm{T} \longrightarrow {}_n\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow {}_n\mathrm{A} \longrightarrow 0. \] A classical theorem of Weil (A. Weil: Variétés abéliennes et courbes algébriques, §IX th. 33 cor. 1) tells us that \({}_n\mathrm{A}\) is a finite group defined by a \(k\)-algebra of rank \(n^{2\rho_{ab}}\). Since \({}_n\mathrm{T}\) is a finite group of rank \(n^{\rho_r}\), we deduce the announced structure of \({}_n\mathrm{G}\).

Now let H be the smallest closed subscheme of G that dominates \({}_{q^n}\mathrm{G}\) for every \(n\). It follows from the above and from Exp. XV 4.6 that H is a smooth and connected subgroup, hence of the same type as G. Raising to the \(n\)-th power in H is an epimorphism (because \({}_q\mathrm{H}\) is finite), so that we have the exact sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow {}_q\mathrm{H} \longrightarrow {}_q\mathrm{G} \longrightarrow {}_q(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}) \longrightarrow 0. \] It follows that \({}_q(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}) = 0\), hence \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} = 0\). That is to say, the subgroups \({}_{q^n}\mathrm{G}\) are schematically dense in G.

Continuation of the proof of 1.6. i) To show that G is commutative, one reduces by the usual procedure to the case where S is noetherian, then to the case where S is the spectrum of a local ring with closed point \(s\). The centre of G is representable by a closed group subscheme Z of G (Exp. XI 6.11). To show that \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{G}\), it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{G}\) after reduction by every power of the maximal ideal of the ring of S (for Z will then be an open subgroup of G, hence will be equal to G, since G has connected fibres). This reduces us to the case where S is local artinian.

Let \(q\) be an integer invertible on S. For every integer \(n\), \({}_{q^n}\mathrm{G}_s\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), étale and central (1.7). Since G is smooth, \({}_{q^n}(\mathrm{G}_s)\) lifts to an étale and central S-group subscheme \(\mathrm{M}_n\) of G (Exp. XV 1.2). The family of flat subgroups \(\mathrm{M}_n\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), is then schematically dense in G (1.7 and EGA IV 11.10.9). Since Z is closed in G and dominates all the \(\mathrm{M}_n\), we have \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{G}\).

1.6 ii). To see that \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is flat and quasi-finite over S, it suffices to show that raising to the \(n\)-th power in G is a flat and quasi-finite morphism. Since G is flat over S, one is reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field (EGA IV 11.3.10), and we remarked in the proof of 1.7 that raising to the \(n\)-th power was an epimorphism. Moreover, \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) is separated over S, because G is separated over S (Exp. VIB 5.2). For \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) to be finite over S, it is then necessary and sufficient that the fibres of \({}_n\mathrm{G}\) be the spectra of finite algebras of locally constant rank (one sees this immediately by reducing to the case where S is the spectrum of a henselian local ring). But, by 1.7, this condition is equivalent to saying that the abelian rank, or the reductive rank, of the fibres of G is locally constant.

1.6 iii). To see that the family of subgroups \({}_{q^n}\mathrm{G}\) \((n \in \mathbb{N})\) is universally schematically dense in G, one again reduces to the case S noetherian. Taking into account 1.7 and 1.6 ii), it then suffices to apply EGA IV 11.10.9.

Proof of 1.4 b). We have reduced to the case where S was the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring and where \(\mathrm{G}^0\) possessed a Cartan subgroup C. Let \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}\,\mathrm{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}\,\mathrm{T}\), where T is the unique maximal torus of C (Exp. XII 7.1 a) and b)). Since H is separated over S, it follows from 1.6 ii) and from Exp. VIII 7.12 that the restriction of \(u\) to C is a closed immersion. On the other hand, to prove that \(u\) is a closed immersion, it suffices to show that this is so for \(u|_{\mathrm{N}}\) (1.3 b)). Since by hypothesis \(\mathrm{W} = \mathrm{N}/\mathrm{C}\) is representable by a finite group scheme, this will follow from the following lemma, applied to the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N} \longrightarrow \mathrm{W} \longrightarrow 1 \] (note that a proper immersion is a closed immersion).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.8

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, an extension of a group prescheme \(\mathrm{G}''\), proper and of finite presentation over S, by a group prescheme \(\mathrm{G}'\), of finite presentation and flat over S: \[ 1 \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}' \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}'' \longrightarrow 1. \] Let moreover H be an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S (or locally of finite presentation if S is locally noetherian) and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-group morphism. Then if the restriction of \(u\) to \(\mathrm{G}'\) is proper, \(u\) is proper.

Proof of 1.8. We reduce as usual to the case where S is noetherian (Exp. VIB §10 and Exp. XV 6.2) and we can then apply the valuative criterion of properness (EGA II 7.3.8). We therefore assume that S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, with closed point \(s\) and generic point \(t\). Let \(\overline{x} \in \mathrm{G}(t)\) and \(h \in \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{S})\) be such that \(u_t(\overline{x}) = h(t)\). We must show that \(\overline{x}\) comes from a unique element \(x\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\). It even suffices to prove the existence and uniqueness of \(x\) after a faithfully flat extension of the discrete valuation ring A. Now let \(\overline{y}\) be the projection of \(\overline{x}\) into \(\mathrm{G}''(t)\). Since \(\mathrm{G}''\) is proper over S, \(\overline{y}\) comes from a unique element \(y\) of \(\mathrm{G}''(\mathrm{S})\). Let X be the inverse image of \(y\) in G. The prescheme X is faithfully flat over S (because \(\mathrm{G}'\) is faithfully flat over S, as is the morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}''\) (Exp. VIB §9)). After a faithfully flat change of discrete valuation ring, we may assume that X possesses a section \(\ell\) over S (EGA IV 14.5.8). Replacing \(\overline{x}\) by \(\overline{x}\ell_t^{-1}\), we may assume that \(\overline{x} \in \mathrm{G}'(t)\). But then the existence and uniqueness of \(x\) follow from the fact that \(u|_{\mathrm{G}'}\) is proper.
Proof of 1.5.

a) We shall see in Exp. XIX that if G is reductive, G possesses maximal tori locally for the faithfully flat topology, has a finite Weyl group, and null unipotent rank. Assertion a) follows, taking 1.4 into account.

b) If G has affine fibres, of null unipotent rank, G possesses maximal tori locally for the fpqc topology (Exp. XV 8.18). It then suffices to apply 1.4, taking into account the fact that since G has connected, smooth, affine, solvable fibres, its Weyl group is the unit group (BIBLE 6 th. 1 cor. 3).
Proof of 1.3 a).

Since the morphism \(u\) is already a monomorphism, to see that \(u\) is an immersion it suffices to show that \(u\) is proper at the points of \(u(\mathrm{G})\) (EGA IV 15.7.1), and for this we can use the valuative criterion of local properness (EGA IV 15.7.5). We are thus reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring, with algebraically closed residue field, with closed point \(s\) and generic point \(t\). Since G is flat over S, standard reductions (cf. Exp. VIII proof of 7.1) allow us to reduce to the case where H is flat over S, where \(u_t\) is an isomorphism and where \(u_s\) is an isomorphism on the underlying spaces. Saying that \(u\) is an immersion is then equivalent to saying that \(u\) is an isomorphism.
By hypothesis, the group G possesses Cartan subgroups locally for the fpqc topology, so we can speak of the open set U of regular points of G (Exp. XV 7.3 i) and ii)).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.9

With the preceding hypotheses, in order for \(u\) to be an immersion, it suffices that \(u|_{\mathrm{U}}\) be an immersion.

Indeed, saying that \(u|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is an immersion means that there exist an open set V of H and a closed set F of V such that \(u|_{\mathrm{U}}\) factors through F and induces an isomorphism of U onto F. Since \(\mathrm{H}_t\), hence also \(\mathrm{V}_t\), is irreducible, we necessarily have \(\mathrm{V}_t = \mathrm{F}_t\). But then \(\mathrm{F}_t\) dominates the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{V}_t\) in V, which is equal to V since H is flat over S. In short, we have \(\mathrm{V} = \mathrm{F}\). It follows that \(u|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is an open immersion, hence \(u\) is an open immersion (VIB 2.6).

It therefore remains for us to show that \(u|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is an immersion, and for this let us apply the valuative criterion of local properness. After replacing S by the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, faithfully flat over S, we must show that if \(h\) is a section of H over S whose image \(h(\mathrm{S})\) is contained in \(u(\mathrm{U})\), then \(h\) is the image of a section \(g\) of U over S. It suffices to show that \(h\) is contained in the image of a Cartan subgroup C of G. Indeed, by hypothesis, \(u|_{\mathrm{C}}\) is an immersion, hence \(h\) is the image of a section \(g\) of C, which is necessarily a section of U.

Let \(a \in \mathrm{U}(s)\) (resp. \(b \in \mathrm{U}(t)\)) be such that \(u_s(a) = h(s)\) (resp. \(u_t(b) = h(t)\)). Since \(u_t\) is an isomorphism, \(h(t)\) is a regular point of \(\mathrm{H}_t\), hence is contained in a unique Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm{D}_t\) of \(\mathrm{H}_t\) (Exp. XIII 3.2). Let D be the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{D}_t\) in H.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.10

i) \(\mathrm{D}_s\) is a nilpotent algebraic group (Exp. VIB §8).

ii) \(\dim \mathrm{D}_s = \nu =\) nilpotent rank of \(\mathrm{G} =\) nilpotent rank of \((\mathrm{H}_s)_{\text{réd}}\).

iii) \(h(s) \in \mathrm{D}_s(\kappa(s))\).

i) Since H is separated (Exp. VIB 5.2), the same holds for D. Moreover, D is flat over S, and its generic fibre is nilpotent. The fact that \(\mathrm{D}_s\) is nilpotent then follows from Exp. VIB 8.4.

ii) Since \((\mathrm{H}_s)_{\text{réd}} \simeq \mathrm{G}_s\), these two groups have the same nilpotent rank. The group G possessing Cartan subgroups locally for the fpqc topology, \(\mathrm{G}_s\) and \(\mathrm{G}_t\) have the same nilpotent rank \(\nu\). Finally, the group D being flat over S, we have \(\dim \mathrm{D}_s = \dim \mathrm{D}_t = \nu\) (Exp. VIB §4).
iii) Since \(h_t\) factors through \(\mathrm{D}_t\), \(h\) obviously factors through D, whence iii).
This being so, the following lemma proves that \((\mathrm{D}_s)_{\text{réd}}\) is a Cartan subgroup of \((\mathrm{H}_s)_{\text{réd}} \simeq \mathrm{G}_s\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.11

Let G be a smooth and connected algebraic group defined over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and D a smooth, nilpotent algebraic subgroup containing a regular element \(a\) of \(\mathrm{G}(k)\). Then \(\mathrm{D}^0\) is contained in a Cartan subgroup of G. If moreover \(\dim \mathrm{D}\) is equal to the nilpotent rank of G, then D is a Cartan subgroup of G (hence is connected).

Let Z be the centre of G, \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\), which is affine (Exp. XII 6.1), and \(a'\), \(\mathrm{D}'\) the images of \(a\), D in \(\mathrm{G}'\). It follows immediately from the correspondence between Cartan subgroups of G and Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XII 6.6 e)) that \(a'\) is a regular element of \(\mathrm{G}'\) and that it suffices to prove the lemma for the pair \((\mathrm{D}', \mathrm{G}')\); this allows us to assume G affine.

Let then \(s\) be the semisimple component of \(a\), which belongs to \(\mathrm{D}(k)\) (BIBLE 4 th. 3) and is regular (BIBLE 7 th. 2 cor. 1). By BIBLE 6 th. 3, \(s\) centralizes \(\mathrm{D}^0\), hence \(\mathrm{D}^0\) is contained in the connected centralizer of \(s\), which is a Cartan subgroup of G (BIBLE 7 th. 2). If now \(\dim \mathrm{D} =\) nilpotent rank of G, then \(\mathrm{D}^0\) is a Cartan subgroup of G, equal to \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\), where T is the unique maximal torus of \(\mathrm{D}^0\) (Exp. XII 6.6). But D is nilpotent, hence centralizes T (BIBLE 6 th. 2), hence \(\mathrm{D} = \mathrm{D}^0\).

Let us now work with the group G. The group \(\mathrm{C}_t = u_t^{-1}(\mathrm{D}_t)\) is the unique Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) that contains \(b\). Let C be the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{C}_t\) in G.

By functoriality of the schematic closure, \(u|_{\mathrm{C}}\) factors through D, hence \(u_s(\mathrm{C}_s) \subset \mathrm{D}_s\). Since \(u_s\) is an isomorphism of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) onto \((\mathrm{H}_s)_{\text{réd}}\), since \(\dim \mathrm{C}_s = \dim \mathrm{C}_t = \nu = \dim \mathrm{D}_s\) (1.10), and since \(\mathrm{D}_s\) is connected (1.11), \(u_s\) provides an isomorphism of \((\mathrm{C}_s)_{\text{réd}}\) onto \((\mathrm{D}_s)_{\text{réd}}\), hence \((\mathrm{C}_s)_{\text{réd}}\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). The following lemma shows that in fact C is a Cartan subgroup of G.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.12

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, G a smooth S-group prescheme of finite type, and C a group subprescheme of G, flat over S, such that the generic fibre \(\mathrm{C}_t\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) and the reduced geometric closed fibre \((\mathrm{C}_{\overline{s}})_{\text{réd}}\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\). Then C is a Cartan subgroup of G.

We must show that C is smooth over S, and it suffices to establish this point after a faithfully flat extension of the base. The hypotheses made on C imply that the nilpotent rank of the fibres of G is constant, and we can therefore speak of the open set U of regular points of G (Exp. XV 7.3). Since \((\mathrm{C}_{\overline{s}})_{\text{réd}}\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\), \(\mathrm{C}_{\overline{s}}\) contains a regular point \(a_{\overline{s}}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\). But C is flat over S, hence (EGA IV 14.5.8), after a faithfully flat extension of the base, we may assume that \(a_{\overline{s}}\) is an element of \(\mathrm{G}(s)\) and lifts to a section \(a\) of \(\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{S})\). Since U is open and contains \(a(s)\), U contains \(a\). Let \(\mathrm{C}'\) be the unique Cartan subgroup of G containing \(a\) (Exp. XV 7.3). Necessarily \(\mathrm{C}_t = \mathrm{C}'_t\), and consequently \(\mathrm{C}'\) and C coincide with the identity component of the schematic closure of \(\mathrm{C}_t\) in G (note that \(\mathrm{C}'\) and C are flat over S).

End of the proof of 1.3 a). By hypothesis, the restriction of \(u\) to the Cartan subgroup C of G is an immersion. It is clear that \(h(\mathrm{S})\) is contained in \(u(\mathrm{C}) = \mathrm{D}\), hence \(h\) is the image of a section \(g\) of \(\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{S})\). Q.E.D.
Proof of 1.3 b).

We shall again use the valuative criterion of local properness (EGA IV 15.5) in the case of an immersion (resp. the valuative criterion of properness (EGA II 7.3.6) in the case of a closed immersion). This reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field. Let \(s\) be the closed point and \(t\) the generic point of S. Replacing H by the schematic closure in H of \(u_t(\mathrm{G}_t)\), we may assume that H is flat over S and that \(u_t\) is an isomorphism.

Let \(\mathrm{G}^0\) be the connected component of G. By 1.3 a), \(u|_{\mathrm{G}^0}\) is an immersion; denote by \(\mathrm{H}'\) the group subprescheme of H which is the image of \(\mathrm{G}^0\). We thus have \(\mathrm{H}'_t = (\mathrm{H}_t)^0_{\text{réd}}\).

To verify the valuative criterion, we must show that every section \(h\) of H over S such that \(h(\mathrm{S})\) is contained in \(u(\mathrm{G})\), in the case of an immersion (resp. every section \(h\) of H over S, in the case of a closed immersion), is the image of a section \(g\) of G over S.

Let C be a Cartan subgroup of G and \(\mathrm{D} = u(\mathrm{C})\) the Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}'\) which is the image of C. The S-group \(\mathrm{D}' = \mathrm{int}(h)\mathrm{D}\) has connected fibres, hence its underlying space is contained in that of \(\mathrm{H}'\); on the other hand, being smooth over S, it is reduced, and consequently \(\mathrm{D}'\) is a smooth group subprescheme of \(\mathrm{H}'\). The fibres of \(\mathrm{D}'\) are Cartan subgroups of the fibres of \(\mathrm{H}'\), hence \(\mathrm{D}'\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{H}'\), hence \(\mathrm{C}' = u^{-1}(\mathrm{D}')\) is a Cartan subgroup of G. But \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}')\) is smooth and surjective over S (Exp. XII 7.1 b)). Since S is henselian with algebraically closed residue field, there exists \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S})\) such that \(\mathrm{int}(g)\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{C}'\). After replacing \(h\) by \(u(g)^{-1}h\), we may assume that \(h(t)\) normalizes \(\mathrm{D}_t\) (and that \(h(s)\) is the image of an element of the normalizer of \(\mathrm{C}_s\) in \(\mathrm{G}_s\), in the case of an immersion). Let \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{C})\). By hypothesis \(u|_{\mathrm{N}}\) is an immersion (resp. a closed immersion), hence \(h\) is indeed the image of a section of N over S, which completes the proof.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: S. Koizumi & G. Shimura, Specialization of abelian varieties, Sci. Papers Coll. Gen. Ed. Univ. Tokyo 9 (1959), 187-211.

blocks.html: 2. A representability theorem for quotients
Let us "recall" the following result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let S be a prescheme, X and Y two S-preschemas, and \(f : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) an S-morphism. Suppose we are in one of the two following cases:

a) The morphism \(f\) is locally of finite presentation.

b) The prescheme S is locally noetherian and X is locally of finite type over S.

Then the following conditions are equivalent:

i) There exist an S-prescheme \(\mathrm{X}'\) and a factorization of \(f\): \[ f : \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\ f'\ } \mathrm{X}' \xrightarrow{\ f''\ } \mathrm{Y}, \] where \(f'\) is a faithfully flat S-morphism locally of finite presentation and \(f''\) is a monomorphism.

ii) The (first) projection: \[ p_1 : \mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{Y}}{\times} \mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X} \] is a flat morphism.

Moreover, if the preceding conditions are fulfilled, \((\mathrm{X}', f')\) is a quotient of X by the equivalence relation defined by \(f\) (for the fpqc topology), so that the factorization \(f = f'' \circ f'\) of i) is unique up to isomorphism.

The proof of this delicate theorem will be found in EGA V; one may also consult the exposé of J.-P. Murre, Séminaire Bourbaki, Mai 1965, N°294 th. 2 cor. 2, where the case Y locally noetherian, X of finite type over Y, is treated. We are going to see that one can reduce to this case.
Let us first make a few remarks:

a) i) \(\Rightarrow\) ii) is trivial. Indeed, the first projection: \[ p'_1 : \mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{X}'}{\times} \mathrm{X} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X} \] factors through \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{Y}} \mathrm{X}\): \[ p'_1 : \mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{X}'}{\times} \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\ u\ } \mathrm{X} \underset{\mathrm{Y}}{\times} \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\ p_1\ } \mathrm{X}. \] The morphism \(u\) is an isomorphism, since \(f''\) is a monomorphism, and \(p'_1\) is flat, since \(f'\) is flat, hence \(p_1\) is flat.
b) The assertions of 2.1 are local on Y (hence are local on S); they are also local on X, as follows easily from the fact that a flat morphism locally of finite presentation is open (EGA IV 11.3.1).

c) Under the hypotheses of 2.1 a), in view of the above, we are reduced to the case where X and Y are affine and \(f\) is of finite presentation. After replacing S by Y, we may assume X and Y of finite presentation over S. One then reduces to the case S noetherian thanks to EGA IV 11.2.6.

d) Under the hypotheses of 2.1 b), we may assume S, X, Y affine, S noetherian and X of finite type over S. Consider Y as a filtered projective limit of affine schemes \(\mathrm{Y}_i\) of finite type over S. The schemes \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{Y}_i} \mathrm{X}\) form a decreasing filtered family of closed subschemes of \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}\), whose projective limit is \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{Y}} \mathrm{X}\). Since \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}\) is noetherian, we have \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{Y}_i} \mathrm{X} = \mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{Y}} \mathrm{X}\) for \(i\) large enough, so that \(f_i : \mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{f} \mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{Y}_i\) satisfies the hypotheses of 2.1 ii) if \(f\) does. Since the equivalence relation defined by \(f\) on X coincides with the one defined by \(f_i\), it is clear that it suffices to prove ii) \(\Rightarrow\) i) for \(f_i\), which reduces us to the case where Y is of finite type over S.
Application to group preschemes

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme locally of finite presentation over S, acting on an S-prescheme X. Let \(\xi \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\) be a section of X over S such that the stabilizer H of \(\xi\) in G is a group subprescheme of G, flat over S. Then, if X is locally of finite type over S, or if S is locally noetherian, the quotient homogeneous space \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is representable by an S-prescheme, locally of finite presentation over S, and the S-morphism: \[ f : \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \mathrm{X}, \qquad g \mapsto g \cdot \xi. \] factors as: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{G} \ar[d]_-{p} \ar[dr]^-{f} & \\ \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H} \ar[r]_-{i} & \mathrm{X} } \] where \(p\) is the canonical projection, which is a faithfully flat morphism locally of finite presentation, and \(i\) is a monomorphism. (To fix ideas, we have assumed that G acts on X on the left.)

Proof. The morphism \(f\) makes G an X-prescheme. By definition of the stabilizer of \(\xi\), the morphism: \[ \mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{X}}{\times} \mathrm{G}, \qquad (g, h) \mapsto (g, gh) \] is an isomorphism. Since H is flat over S, \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{H}\) is flat over G, hence the first projection \(p_1 : \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{X}} \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a flat morphism. Moreover, if X is locally of finite type over S, \(f\) is locally of finite presentation (EGA IV 1.4.3 v)), and otherwise S is assumed locally noetherian. It then suffices to apply 2.1 to the morphism \(f\).
It remains to see that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}\) is locally of finite presentation over S, but this follows immediately from Exp. V 9.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let S be a prescheme, G and H two S-group preschemes, and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-homomorphism. Suppose G is locally of finite presentation over S and that either H is locally of finite type over S, or S is locally noetherian. Then, if \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{Ker}(u)\) is flat over S, the quotient group \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{K}\) is representable by an S-group prescheme locally of finite presentation over S, and \(u\) factors as: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{G} \ar[rr]^-{u} \ar[dr]_-{p} & & \mathrm{H} \\ & \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{K} \ar[ur]_-{i} & } \] where \(p\) is the canonical projection and \(i\) a monomorphism.
Proof: one applies 2.2, taking \(\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{H}\) and for \(\xi\) the unit section of H.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, smooth over S, H an S-group prescheme, smooth over S, with connected fibres (hence of finite presentation over S, by VIB 5.5), and \(i : \mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}\) a monomorphism of S-groups, so that H is a subgroup of G. Suppose that \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H})\) (which is representable by a closed group subprescheme of G, of finite presentation over S (Exp. XI 6.11)) is flat over S. Then \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is representable by an S-prescheme, of finite presentation over S and quasi-projective over S.

All the assertions to be proved, except the last one, are local on S. To establish them, we may therefore assume S quasi-compact and the relative dimension of H over S constant and equal to \(r\). Let us proceed as in XV §5. Let, for every integer \(n > 0\), \(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) (resp. \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\)) be the \(n\)-th normal invariant of the unit section of G (resp. of H) (EGA IV 16). The sheaf of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-modules \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) is a quotient of \(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) and, H being smooth over S of dimension \(r\), \(\mathrm{H}^{(n)}\) canonically defines an element \(\xi_n\) of \(\mathrm{X}_n = \mathrm{Grass}_{\varphi(n,r)}\,\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) (EGA I 2nd ed. §9) for a suitable integer \(\varphi(n, r)\). On the other hand, G acts in a natural way on \(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) (hence also on \(\mathrm{X}_n\)) through the representation: \[ \mathrm{G} \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr}}(\mathrm{G}), \qquad g \mapsto \mathrm{int}(g). \] Since S is quasi-compact, there exists an integer \(m\) such that for \(n \geqslant m\) we have: \[ \mathrm{N} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{H}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}\,\mathrm{H}^{(n)} \] (Exp. XI 6.11 b)).

That is to say, N is the stabilizer of \(\xi_n\) for \(n\) large. The representability of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) therefore follows from 2.2. The fact that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is of finite presentation over S is a consequence of Exp. V 9.1. It remains to see that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is quasi-projective over S, and for this we are going to exhibit an invertible sheaf on \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\), S-ample and canonical. Consider the functor \(\mathscr{F} : (\mathbf{Sch}/\mathrm{S})^{\circ} \to \mathbf{Ens}\) such that for every S-prescheme T we have: \[ \mathscr{F}(\mathrm{T}) = \text{set of T-subgroups H of } \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{T}}, \text{ representable, smooth over T, with connected fibres.} \]

Such a group H is of finite presentation over S (Exp. VIB 5.5) and \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{T}}\) is a quasi-affine morphism (EGA IV 8.11.2). By effective descent of quasi-affine morphisms (SGA 1 VIII 7.9), one deduces that \(\mathscr{F}\) is a sheaf for the fpqc topology. Since \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is the sheaf associated with a subsheaf of \(\mathscr{F}\), one sees that there exists a canonical monomorphism \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N} \to \mathscr{F}\). There thus exists a subgroup \(\mathrm{H}'\) of \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} (\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N})\), representable, smooth over \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\), with connected fibres, "universal" for the functor \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\). I claim that the invertible sheaf \(\mathscr{L} = (\det(\mathrm{Lie}\,\mathrm{H}'))^{-1}\) is S-ample. In this form, the assertion becomes local on S and the proof is analogous to the one given in (Exp. XV 5.8).
The following corollary was announced in Exp. XIV 4.8 bis.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres, and P a parabolic subgroup of G (Exp. XIV 4.8 bis). Then \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{P}\) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and projective over S.

It only remains to show (loc. cit.) that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{P}\) is representable by a quasi-projective S-prescheme of finite presentation, which follows from 2.4, taking into account the fact that \(\mathrm{P} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{P})\) (loc. cit.), hence is flat over S.
3. Groups with flat centre

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme smooth and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres. Suppose that the centre Z of G (which is representable by Exp. XI 6.11) is flat over S. For every integer \(n > 0\), denote by \(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}\) the locally free \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-module equal to the \(n\)-th normal invariant of G along the unit section, and let \(\rho_n\) be the natural "adjoint representation" of G in \(\mathrm{GL}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{G}^{(n)})\). Then:

a) The quotient \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is representable by an S-group prescheme, smooth, of finite presentation over S, quasi-affine, with affine and connected fibres.

b) The representation \(\rho_n\) factors through \(\mathrm{G}'\): \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{G} \ar[rr]^-{\rho_n} \ar[dr] & & \mathrm{GL}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{G}^{(n)}) \\ & \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z} \ar[ur]_-{i_n} & } \] If S is quasi-compact, \(i_n\) is a monomorphism for \(n\) large enough.

c) The functor \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{G}'}\) of subtori of \(\mathrm{G}'\) is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth over S, which is a sum of a family of preschemes affine over S if S is quasi-compact.

Proof. The assertions contained in a) are local on S, which allows us to assume S quasi-compact. By Exp. XI 6.11 b), Z is equal to \(\mathrm{Ker}\,\rho_n\) for \(n\) large enough, so the latter is flat over S. The fact that \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is representable and that \(i_n\) is a monomorphism thus follows from 2.3. Since G is smooth and of finite presentation over S, the same holds for \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. VIB §9). The group \(\mathrm{GL}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{G}^{(n)})\) is affine over S, and a monomorphism of finite presentation is quasi-affine (EGA IV 8.11.2), hence \(\mathrm{G}'\) is quasi-affine over S with affine fibres. Since \(\mathrm{G}'\) is smooth over S, the functor \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{G}'}\) is formally smooth (Exp. XI 2.1 bis). Taking into account Exp. XI 4.6 and 4.3, the assertions contained in 3.1 c) will thus follow from the next lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2

Let S be a prescheme, G and H two S-group preschemes of finite presentation over S, \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-group monomorphism, and \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{G}}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{H}}\)) the functors of subtori of G (resp. of H) (cf. Exp. XV §8). Then the map \(\mathrm{T} \mapsto u(\mathrm{T})\) defines a monomorphism (XV 8.3 c)) \[ \widetilde{u} : \mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{G}} \longrightarrow \mathscr{T}_{\mathrm{H}} \] which is representable by a closed immersion of finite presentation.

By the usual procedure, we are reduced to the following problem: let T be a subtorus of H, \(\mathrm{T}' = \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{H}\) its inverse image in G, and \(u_{\mathrm{T}} : \mathrm{T}' \to \mathrm{T}\) the S-group monomorphism deduced from \(u\). One must show that the S-functor \(\prod_{\mathrm{T}'/\mathrm{T}}(\mathrm{T}'/\mathrm{T})\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of S, of finite presentation. But T, being a torus, is smooth over S with connected fibres, and it suffices to apply Exp. XI 6.10.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres, and Z the centre of G. Suppose that Z is flat over S and that the unipotent rank (Exp. XV 6.1 ter) of G is equal to that of Z. Then:

a) The group \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is representable, and if S is quasi-compact, the canonical morphism \(i_n : \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{GL}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{G}^{(n)})\) (cf. 3.1 b)) is an immersion for \(n\) large.

b) The group \(\mathrm{G}'\) is quasi-affine over S, with affine fibres, the centre of \(\mathrm{G}'\) is the unit group, and \(\mathrm{G}'\) is of null unipotent rank.

c) The group \(\mathrm{G}'\) possesses maximal tori locally for the étale topology, and these are also Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm{G}'\). The functor \(\mathscr{TM}_{\mathrm{G}'}\) of maximal tori of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XV §8) is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and affine over S.

d) The group G possesses Cartan subgroups locally for the étale topology, and the functor \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathrm{G}}\) of Cartan subgroups of G is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth and affine over S.

Proof. By 3.1, the group \(\mathrm{G}'\) is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth and quasi-affine over S, with affine fibres. In view of the correspondence between the Cartan subgroups of the fibres of G and of the fibres of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XII 6.6 e)), the hypothesis made on the unipotent rank of Z implies that \(\mathrm{G}'\) has null unipotent rank. Using now Exp. XV 8.18, one sees that \(\mathrm{G}'\) possesses maximal tori locally for the étale topology. The fact that \(i_n\) is an immersion for \(n\) large then follows from 3.1 b) and 1.4 a). This completes the proof of a).

Let us now show c). Since \(\mathrm{G}'\) is of null unipotent rank, it is clear that every maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}'\) is also a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\). The functor of maximal tori of \(\mathrm{G}'\) is representable by a subprescheme that is both open and closed in the functor of subtori of \(\mathrm{G}'\); moreover, it is of finite type over S (Exp. XV 8.15); it then follows from 3.1 c) that this functor is representable by an S-prescheme, smooth and affine over S.

Since \(\mathrm{G}'\) possesses Cartan subgroups locally for the étale topology, the same holds for G, and the functor of Cartan subgroups of G is canonically isomorphic to that of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XV 7.3 iv)), hence c) \(\Rightarrow\) d).

It remains for us to show b), and more precisely, it remains to prove that the centre \(\mathrm{Z}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}'\) is the unit group. Since \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is representable (Exp. XI 6.11) and of finite presentation over S, it suffices to show that the fibres of \(\mathrm{Z}'\) are reduced to the unit group (Exp. VIB 2.9), which reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. Moreover, \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is obviously contained in every Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'\), hence in every maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}'\) by c), hence is of multiplicative type. On the other hand, we shall see in Exp. XVII that if G is a connected algebraic group with centre Z, the centre \(\mathrm{Z}'\) of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is unipotent. In the present case, \(\mathrm{Z}'\), being both of multiplicative type and unipotent, is reduced to the unit group (cf. Exp. XVII).
Examples of groups whose centre is flat

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S, smooth, with connected fibres, and Z the centre of G. Then Z is flat over S in the two following cases:

a) The unipotent rank \(\rho_u\) (Exp. XV 6.1 ter) of the fibres of G is null.

b) i) S is reduced.

ii) The dimension of the fibres of Z is a locally constant function on S.

iii) The unipotent rank of G is equal to the unipotent rank of Z.
Proof of 3.4 a). We are going to prove at the same time the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5

Under the hypotheses of 3.4 a), suppose moreover that G possesses a maximal torus T, and let \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) be the Cartan subgroup of G associated with T (Exp. XII 7.1). Then:

(i) \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of G.

(ii) C is commutative and equal to \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{Z}\).

(iii) If the quotients \(\mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) and \(\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) are representable, they are representable by abelian preschemes (i.e. S-group preschemes, smooth over S, whose fibres are abelian varieties) and the canonical monomorphism \(\mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) is an isomorphism.

Using the general limit-passage properties proved in Exp. VIB §10 and Exp. XV 6.2, 6.3, and 6.3 bis, one reduces as usual to the case where S is noetherian (note that the assertions contained in 3.4 and 3.5 are local on S). We remarked in 3.1 that the hypotheses made on G imply that Z is representable. To show that Z is flat over S, one then reduces, by EGA 0III 10.2.6, to the case where S is local artinian. But then, G being smooth, G possesses maximal tori locally for the étale topology (Exp. XV 8.17). After a finite flat extension of S (which is permissible for proving that Z is flat), we may therefore assume that G possesses a maximal torus T.
Let us show, in the same way, that to establish 3.5, we may reduce to the case S artinian.

i) Since Z is closed in G (Exp. XI 6.11), \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) is a closed group subscheme of T. It then follows from Exp. X 4.8 b) that \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) is of multiplicative type if and only if it is flat over S. As above, it suffices to establish that \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) is flat when S is artinian.

ii) Since G is of null unipotent rank, every Cartan subgroup C of G satisfies the hypotheses of Lemma 1.6, hence is commutative. The fact that \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{Z}\) will follow from iii).

iii) If \(\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) is representable, \(\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) is smooth over S (Exp. VIB 9) and its fibres are abelian varieties (1.7), hence \(\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) is an abelian prescheme. To show that the canonical monomorphism \[ \mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T} \] is an isomorphism, it suffices to verify it when S is local artinian. Indeed, one will deduce successively that \(\mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) is flat over S (EGA 0III 10.2.6), then that \(i\) is flat (EGA IV 11.3.10), then that \(i\) is an open immersion (EGA IV 17.9.1), then that \(i\) is an isomorphism (because \(\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) has connected fibres).

We assume from now on that S is local artinian with closed point \(s\) and that G possesses a maximal torus T. Let \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\). The group C is a Cartan subgroup of G (Exp. XII 7.1) and dominates Z.

The algebraic group \(\mathrm{M}_s = \mathrm{Z}_s \cap \mathrm{T}_s\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) which is central. Since T is smooth over S, \(\mathrm{M}_s\) lifts to a group subscheme M of T, of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 3.6 bis) and contained in the centre of G (Exp. IX 3.9), hence contained in \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\). Since S is artinian and M and T are flat over S, the quotient groups \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}/\mathrm{M}\), \(\mathrm{Z}' = \mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{M}\) and \(\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T}\) are representable (Exp. VIA §§4 and 5). By construction, \((\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}/\mathrm{M})_s\) is the unit group, hence \(\mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T} = \mathrm{M}\) (Exp. VIB 2.9); a fortiori, it is flat over S, which proves 3.5. i).

By passage to the quotient, we have a canonical monomorphism \(i : \mathrm{Z}' \to \mathrm{A}\), which is therefore a closed immersion (Exp. VIB 1.4.2). We have already remarked that A is an abelian scheme. It remains to show that \(i\) is an isomorphism. Indeed, this will prove 3.5 iii) and will imply that \(\mathrm{Z}'\) is flat over S, hence that Z is flat over S (as an extension of \(\mathrm{Z}'\) by the flat group M (Exp. VIB §9)). Since \(\mathrm{Z}_s\) has the same abelian rank as \(\mathrm{G}_s\) (Exp. XII 6.1), \(i_s\) is an epimorphism, hence is an isomorphism. Let \(q\) be an integer \(> 0\), invertible on S. By the density theorem of 1.6 iii), to see that \(i(\mathrm{Z}')\) dominates A, it suffices to show that for every integer \(n\) equal to a power of \(q\), \(i(\mathrm{Z}')\) dominates \({}_n\mathrm{A}\). Now let \(\mathrm{M}_s^0\) be the identity component of \(\mathrm{M}_s\). It is immediate by duality that raising to the \(q\)-th power in \(\mathrm{M}_s^0\) is an epimorphism. One deduces immediately that if \(m_0\) is the exponent of \(q\) in the prime factorization of \(\mathrm{card}(\mathrm{M}_s/\mathrm{M}_s^0)\), the image of \({}_{nm_0}\mathrm{Z}_s\) in \(\mathrm{A}_s \simeq \mathrm{Z}_s/\mathrm{M}_s\) dominates \({}_n\mathrm{A}_s\). There thus exists a subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}_s(n)\) of \(\mathrm{Z}_s\) whose image in \(\mathrm{A}_s\) is \({}_n\mathrm{A}_s\). As above, one sees that \(\mathrm{M}_s(n)\) lifts to a central subgroup of G of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}(n)\). The image of \(\mathrm{M}(n)\) in A is a subgroup of multiplicative type (Exp. IX 6.8), hence necessarily equal to \({}_n\mathrm{A}\), since this is so on the reduced fibre (Exp. IX 5.1 bis). This completes the proof of 3.4 a) and of 3.5.

Proof of 3.4 b). The assertion to be proved is local on S, so we may assume S affine with ring A. Considering A as an inductive limit of its sub-\(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras of finite type, one reduces as above to the case where S is noetherian and reduced.

To show that Z is flat, one then has at one's disposal a valuative criterion of flatness (EGA IV 11.8.1), which allows us to reduce to the case where S is the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring with algebraically closed residue field, with generic point \(t\) and closed point \(s\). Let \(\mathrm{Z}'\) be the schematic closure in Z of \(\mathrm{Z}_t\). We must show that \(\mathrm{Z}' = \mathrm{Z}\), and it even suffices to show that \(\mathrm{Z}_s = \mathrm{Z}'_s\) (Exp. VIB 2.6). By (Exp. VIB §4), we have the inequalities: \[ \dim \mathrm{Z}_t = \dim \mathrm{Z}'_t = \dim \mathrm{Z}'_s \leqslant \dim \mathrm{Z}_s. \]

But by hypothesis, \(\dim \mathrm{Z}_t = \dim \mathrm{Z}_s\), hence \(\dim \mathrm{Z}'_s = \dim \mathrm{Z}_s\), and consequently \(\mathrm{Z}'_s\) dominates \((\mathrm{Z}_s)^0_{\text{réd}}\). It follows that \(\mathrm{G}'_s = \mathrm{G}_s/\mathrm{Z}'_s\) is affine (Exp. XII 6.1). In view of the correspondence between Cartan subgroups of G and of \(\mathrm{G}'\) (Exp. XII 6.6 e)), hypothesis 3.4 iii) implies that the unipotent rank of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\) is null, and consequently its Cartan subgroups are also its maximal tori. The image \(\mathrm{Z}''_s\) of \(\mathrm{Z}_s\) in \(\mathrm{G}'_s\) is a central algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\), hence contained in every Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\), that is, in every maximal torus; \(\mathrm{Z}''_s\) is therefore a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm{G}'_s\). Under these conditions, we shall show in (Exp. XVII §7) that there exists a finite subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm{M}_s\) of \(\mathrm{Z}_s\) whose image in \(\mathrm{Z}_s/\mathrm{Z}'_s\) is \(\mathrm{Z}''_s\) (we used this fact in the proof of 3.4 a) when \(\mathrm{Z}''_s\) is étale). Since G is smooth over S and S is the spectrum of a complete local ring, \(\mathrm{M}_s\) lifts to an S-subgroup of multiplicative type M of G (Exp. IX 3.6 bis and Exp. XV 1.6 b)) which is central (Exp. IX 5.6 a)). Hence \(\mathrm{M}_t\) is contained in \(\mathrm{Z}'_t = \mathrm{Z}_t\), and since M is flat, M is contained in \(\mathrm{Z}'\). The group \(\mathrm{M}_s\) is therefore contained in \(\mathrm{Z}'_s\), but this implies that \(\mathrm{Z}''_s\) is the unit group, that is, \(\mathrm{Z}_s = \mathrm{Z}'_s\). This completes the proof of 3.4 b).
Example of a smooth group prescheme with connected fibres whose centre is not flat

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring A, \(\pi\) a uniformizer of A, \(t\) the generic point of S, \(s\) the closed point. Let G be the S-group smooth and affine over S, with ring \(\mathrm{B} = \mathrm{A}[\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{T}^{-1}, \mathrm{U}]/\mathrm{F}\) with \(\mathrm{F} = 1 - \mathrm{T} + \pi\mathrm{U}\), the composition law being defined by: \[ (t, u), (t', u') \longmapsto (tt', \pi u u' + u + u'). \]

The generic fibre \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is thus isomorphic to the multiplicative group \((\mathbb{G}_m)_t\), while the closed fibre \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is isomorphic to the additive group \((\mathbb{G}_a)_s\). The function T is invertible in \(\Gamma(\mathrm{G}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{G}})\) and defines an S-group morphism \(\varphi : \mathrm{G} \to (\mathbb{G}_m)_{\mathrm{S}}\) which is an isomorphism on the generic fibre and the zero morphism on the closed fibre. The datum of \(\varphi\) makes it possible to construct the semi-direct product group \(\mathrm{H} = \mathrm{G} \cdot (\mathbb{G}_m)_{\mathrm{S}}\) (note that \((\mathbb{G}_m)\) acts on \((\mathbb{G}_a)_{\mathrm{S}}\)). The centre Z of H is the unit group on the generic fibre and is equal to \(\mathrm{H}_s\) on the closed fibre, hence is not flat over S.

pages.title: 4. Groups with affine fibres, of null unipotent rank

blocks.html: 4. Groups with affine fibres, of null unipotent rank

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme smooth over S, of finite presentation, with affine, connected fibres, of null unipotent rank (Exp XV 6.1 ter). Then:

a) The centre Z of G is a group of multiplicative type (and consequently is a reductive centre of G (Exp. XII 4.1)).

b) G satisfies conditions a) to d) of 3.3.

c) G possesses maximal tori locally for the étale topology, and these are also Cartan subgroups of G. The functor \(\mathscr{TM}_{\mathrm{G}}\) of maximal tori of G is representable by an S-prescheme smooth and affine over S.

d) G is quasi-affine over S. Moreover, if T is a maximal torus of G, \(\mathrm{N} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}\,\mathrm{T}\) its normalizer in G, and \(\mathrm{W} = \mathrm{N}/\mathrm{T}\) the Weyl group relative to T, the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) G is affine over S.

(ii) \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Z}\) is affine over S.

(iii) N is affine over S.

(iv) W is affine over S.

These conditions are always fulfilled if S is locally noetherian of dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

Proof. Since G has affine fibres (hence of null abelian rank) and null unipotent rank, G possesses maximal tori locally for the étale topology (Exp. XV 8.18), and every maximal torus of G is obviously a Cartan subgroup of G, which proves the first part of c). To see that Z is of multiplicative type, we may assume, by the above, that G possesses a maximal torus T. Since T is also a Cartan subgroup, T dominates Z (for \(\mathrm{T} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) by Exp. XII 7.1) and \(\mathrm{Z} = \mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{T}\) is of multiplicative type by 3.5 i). This proves a). Assertion b) is clear, taking a) into account. On the other hand, the functor \(\mathscr{TM}_{\mathrm{G}}\) is isomorphic to the functor of Cartan subgroups of G (Exp. XII 7.1), hence is smooth and affine over S (3.3 c)), which completes the proof of c). It remains to prove d).
Proof of d). Since \(\mathrm{G}'\) is quasi-affine (3.3 b)) and Z is of multiplicative type, hence affine over S, G is quasi-affine (Exp. VIB §9).
i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) ii). If G is affine, \(\mathrm{G}'\) is affine by Exp. IX 2.3. If \(\mathrm{G}'\) is affine, G is affine as an extension of an affine group by an affine group (Exp. VIB §9).

i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) iii). If G is affine, N is affine since it is closed in G (Exp. XI 6.11 a)). Moreover, \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{N}\) is isomorphic to the functor \(\mathscr{TM}_{\mathrm{G}}\) (conjugacy of maximal tori, cf. Exp. XII 7.1 b)), hence is affine over S by c). Hence if N is affine over S, G is affine, as an "extension" of an affine homogeneous space by an affine group (Exp. VIB §9).
iii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) iv). If N is affine, \(\mathrm{W} = \mathrm{N}/\mathrm{T}\) is affine (Exp. IX 2.3), and conversely by Exp. VIB §9.

Moreover, \(\mathrm{N}/\mathrm{T}\) is representable by an S-group prescheme, étale, separated over S, of finite type (Exp. XV 7.1 iv)), hence quasi-finite over S. The last assertion of d) will thus follow from the next lemma, applied with \(\mathrm{X} = \mathrm{W}\) and \(\mathrm{Y} = \mathrm{S}\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2

Let S be a locally noetherian prescheme of dimension 1, X and Y two S-preschemas locally of finite type over S, and \(u : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) a quasi-finite and separated S-morphism. Suppose that for every point \(s\) of S, the morphism \(u_s : \mathrm{X}_s \to \mathrm{Y}_s\) deduced from \(u\) by the base change \(\mathrm{Spec}\,\kappa(s) \to \mathrm{S}\) is finite. Then \(u\) is an affine morphism.

The assertion to be proved is local on Y, which allows us to assume Y (hence also X) of finite type over S. By EGA IV 8, one sees that it suffices to prove 4.2 when S is the spectrum of a local ring A. By fpqc descent, one may take A complete, then A reduced (EGA II 1.6.4), then A normal (theorem of Nagata (EGA 0IV 22) and theorem of Chevalley (EGA II 6.7.1)). If A is a field, \(u\) is finite by hypothesis, hence affine. Otherwise A is a discrete valuation ring; let \(s\) be the closed point of S, \(t\) the generic point, \(\pi\) a uniformizer of A. Let \(y\) be a point of \(\mathrm{Y}_s\). Applying EGA IV 8 once more, one may replace Y by the spectrum \(\mathrm{Y}'\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{Y},y}\) and X by \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{Y}} \mathrm{Y}'\); finally one may assume \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{Y}',y}\) complete. Since \(u\) is quasi-finite and separated, by EGA II 6.2.6, \(\mathrm{X}'\) is a sum of two schemes \(\mathrm{X}_1\) and \(\mathrm{X}_2\), with \(\mathrm{X}_1\) finite over \(\mathrm{Y}'\) (hence affine) and \(\mathrm{X}_2\) such that \(u(\mathrm{X}_2)\) does not contain the closed point \(y\) of \(\mathrm{Y}'\). I claim that under these conditions \(\mathrm{X}_2\) does not meet the closed fibre \(\mathrm{X}'_s\). Indeed, by hypothesis, \(\mathrm{X}'_s \to \mathrm{Y}'_s\) is finite, hence the restriction of this morphism to the closed set \((\mathrm{X}_2)_s\) is finite and its image in \((\mathrm{Y}')_s\) is a closed set. Since this image does not contain the closed point of the local scheme \((\mathrm{Y}')_s\), \((\mathrm{X}_2)_s\) is empty. Since \(u_t\) is finite, the restriction to \((\mathrm{X}_2)_t = \mathrm{X}_2\) of the morphism \(\mathrm{X}'_t \to \mathrm{Y}'_t\) is finite. Moreover, we have \(\mathrm{Y}'_t = \mathrm{Y}'_{\pi}\), hence the open immersion \(\mathrm{Y}'_t \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is affine. In short, the composite morphism \(\mathrm{X}_2 \to \mathrm{Y}'_t \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is affine, and it indeed follows that the morphism \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X}_1 \amalg \mathrm{X}_2 \to \mathrm{Y}'\) is affine.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation over S, with affine, solvable, connected fibres, of null unipotent rank. Then G is affine over S. If moreover the centre of G is the unit group and if S is quasi-compact, the canonical morphism \(i_n : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{GL}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{G}^{(n)})\) (cf. 3.1 b)) is a closed immersion for \(n\) large enough.

To prove that G is affine over S, we may assume that G possesses a maximal torus T (4.1 c)). The group G having solvable fibres, the Weyl group relative to T is the unit group (BIBLE 6 th. 1 cor. 3) and condition 4.1 d) iv) is satisfied. If the centre of G is the unit group, \(i_n\) is a monomorphism for \(n\) large enough (3.1), hence is a closed immersion (1.5 b)).

pages.title: 5. Application to reductive and semisimple groups

blocks.html: 5. Application to reductive and semisimple groups

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1

An S-group prescheme G is called reductive (resp. semisimple) if G is smooth and affine over S, with reductive (resp. semisimple) fibres.

Reductive groups will be studied systematically from Exp. XIX onwards. In this section, we shall need the following properties, which will be proved in Exp. XIX (without using the developments of the present exposé).

a) Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth and affine over S, with connected fibres, and \(s\) a point of S such that \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is reductive (resp. semisimple). Then there exists a neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{G}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is reductive (resp. semisimple).

b) If G is reductive, G possesses maximal tori locally for the étale topology, and if T is a maximal torus of G, the Weyl group \(\mathrm{W} = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{T}\) is finite over S.
c) A reductive group has null unipotent rank.
Granting this, we propose to improve assertion a) above.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres, and \(s\) a point of S. Then:

(i) If the fibres of G are affine and if \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is reductive, there exists an open neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{G}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is reductive.

(ii) If \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is semisimple, there exists an open neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{G}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is semisimple.
Using Exp. XV 6.2 i) and Exp. VIB §10, one reduces to the case where S is noetherian.

In case ii), consider the centre Z of G, which is representable (Exp. XI 6.11 a)). Since \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is semisimple, it is well known that \(\mathrm{Z}_s\) is finite. Consequently (Exp. VIB §4), there exists a neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that Z is quasi-finite over U. For every point \(t\) of U, \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is then affine (Exp. XII 6.1). After restricting S, we may therefore assume that the fibres of G are affine, in case ii) as in case i).

To prove 5.2 it suffices, taking a) into account, to prove that G is affine over S. Since G has affine fibres, we know that the functor of subtori of G is representable by a smooth S-prescheme (Exp. XV 8.11 and 8.9). After an étale extension covering \(s\), which is permissible, we may therefore assume that there exists a subtorus T of G such that \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). But then, \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) is a smooth subgroup of G, with connected fibres, which dominates T, such that \(\mathrm{C}_s = \mathrm{T}_s\) (for \(\mathrm{C}_s\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) and \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is of null unipotent rank by c)). It follows that \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{T}\), hence T is a Cartan subgroup and a maximal torus of G; a fortiori, the unipotent rank of G is null and one can apply 4.1.

By 4.1 d), it suffices to show that the Weyl group W relative to T is affine over a neighbourhood U of \(s\). In fact we are going to see that W is even finite over a neighbourhood of \(s\). Since W is quasi-finite over S (Exp. XV 7.1 iv)), saying that W is finite over a neighbourhood U of \(s\) is equivalent to saying that the morphism \(\mathrm{W} \to \mathrm{S}\) is proper at \(s\) (EGA IV 15.7 and EGA III 4.4.2), and to establish this point, one has at one's disposal the valuative criterion of local properness (EGA IV 15.7), which reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring with closed point \(s\). But then, by the last assertion of 4.1 d), G is affine over S and one can apply property a) recalled above to conclude that G is reductive (resp. semisimple). Using now property b), one concludes that W is indeed finite over S, which completes the proof of 5.2.

pages.title: 6. Applications: Extension of certain rigidity properties of tori to groups of null unipotent rank

blocks.html: 6. Applications: Extension of certain rigidity properties of tori to groups of null unipotent rank

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.1

Let S be a prescheme and G an S-group prescheme smooth over S, of finite presentation, with connected affine fibres, and whose centre is of multiplicative type (for example G of null unipotent rank, cf. 4.1 a)). Then every closed normal subgroup K of G, of finite presentation over S and quasi-finite over S, is finite over S.

Indeed, for every geometric point \(\overline{s}\) over S, \((\mathrm{K}_{\overline{s}})_{\text{réd}}\) is a finite étale subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\), normal hence central (because \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) is connected). Consequently \(\mathrm{K}' = \mathrm{Z} \cap \mathrm{K}\) (where Z denotes the centre of G) has the same underlying space as K, and it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{K}'\) is finite over S. Now \(\mathrm{K}'\) is a closed, quasi-finite subgroup of the group of multiplicative type Z, hence is finite, as one sees immediately (locally on S, \(\mathrm{K}'\) will be dominated by \({}_n\mathrm{Z}\) for a suitable integer \(n\), and \({}_n\mathrm{Z}\) is finite over S).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.2

Let S and G be as above, K a group subprescheme of G, of finite presentation over S, normal and closed in G, and \(s\) a point of S. If \(\mathrm{K}_s\) is finite (resp. is the unit group), there exists an open neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{K}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is finite over S (resp. is the unit group).

In view of the upper semicontinuity of the dimension of the fibres of K (Exp. VIB §4), we may already assume, after shrinking S, that K is quasi-finite over S, hence finite (6.1). If moreover \(\mathrm{K}_s\) is the unit group, one easily deduces by Nakayama's lemma that K is the unit group over \(\mathrm{Spec}\,\mathscr{O}_s\), hence over a neighbourhood of \(s\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth and of finite presentation over S, with affine, connected fibres, of null unipotent rank, H an S-group prescheme, \(s\) a point of S, and \(u : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) an S-group morphism such that \(\mathrm{Ker}\,u_s\) is central. Suppose moreover that H is of finite presentation over S, or that S is locally noetherian and G locally of finite type over S. Then there exists an open neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that, setting \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{Ker}\,u\), \(\mathrm{K}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) is central, of multiplicative type. Moreover \(\mathrm{G}' = (\mathrm{G}|_{\mathrm{U}})/(\mathrm{K}|_{\mathrm{U}})\) is representable and the morphism \(u' : \mathrm{G}' \to \mathrm{H}|_{\mathrm{U}}\) deduced from \(u\) by passage to the quotient is an immersion.
Proof. Let Z be the centre of G, and \(\mathrm{K}' = \mathrm{K} \cap \mathrm{Z}\).

a) \(\mathrm{K}'\) is a subgroup of multiplicative type over a neighbourhood U of \(s\). Indeed, after an étale extension over a neighbourhood U of \(s\), which is legitimate, we may assume that G possesses a maximal torus T (4.1 c)). But then \(\mathrm{T} \cap \mathrm{K}\) is a group of multiplicative type (Exp. XV 8.3) whose fibre at \(s\) is central by hypothesis, hence \(\mathrm{T} \cap \mathrm{K}\) is central over a neighbourhood of \(s\) (Exp. IX 5.6 a)) and consequently coincides with \(\mathrm{K}'\).

b) Let us show that \(\mathrm{K}' = \mathrm{K}\) over a neighbourhood U of \(s\). By a), we may already assume that \(\mathrm{K}'\) is of multiplicative type, hence flat over S. Since \(\mathrm{K}'_s = \mathrm{K}_s\), the natural immersion \(\mathrm{K}' \to \mathrm{K}\) is then an open immersion over a neighbourhood of \(s\) (Exp. VIB 2.6). If \(t \in \mathrm{U}\), the image of \(\mathrm{K}_t\) in the group \(\mathrm{G}'_t = \mathrm{G}_t/\mathrm{Z}_t\) is thus a finite étale group, normal in \(\mathrm{G}'_t\) (because \(\mathrm{K}_t\) is normal in \(\mathrm{G}_t\)), hence central, hence reduced to the unit group (3.3 b)). That is to say, \(\mathrm{K}_t\) is contained in \(\mathrm{Z}_t\), hence is equal to \(\mathrm{K}'_t\), whence \(\mathrm{K} = \mathrm{K}'\) over U.
c) The representability of \(\mathrm{G}'\) then follows from 2.3; the fact that \(u'\) is an immersion is contained in 1.3 a), taking into account 4.1 c) applied to \(\mathrm{G}'\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.4

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, smooth, of finite presentation over S, with connected fibres, \(s\) a point of S such that \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is generated by its subtori (Exp. XII 8.2) (for example \(\mathrm{G}_s\) affine of null unipotent rank), H an S-group prescheme, and \(u\) and \(v : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) two S-homomorphisms such that \(u_s = v_s\) and such that \(u_s\) is central. Suppose moreover that H is of finite presentation over S, or that S is locally noetherian. Then there exists a neighbourhood U of \(s\) such that \(u|_{\mathrm{U}} = v|_{\mathrm{U}}\).

One reduces as usual to the case where S is noetherian (to study the condition "\(\mathrm{G}_s\) is generated by its subtori", one uses Exp. VIB 7.4). Since G has connected fibres, to show that \(u = v\) over a neighbourhood U of \(s\), it suffices to show that \(u = v\) after reduction by every power of the maximal ideal of the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S},s}\), which reduces us to the case where S is local artinian.

But then, the functor of maximal subtori of G is representable by an S-scheme X, smooth of finite type over S (Exp. XV 8.17). Let T be the maximal subtorus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{X}}\), universal for the functor X. The hypothesis made on \(\mathrm{G}_s\) means that the algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) generated (Exp. VIB §7) by the \(\kappa(s)\)-morphism \(f : \mathrm{T}_s \to \mathrm{G}_s\) (the composite of the immersion \(\mathrm{T}_s \to \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{X}_s}\) and the canonical projection \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{X}_s} \to \mathrm{G}_s\)) is equal to all of \(\mathrm{G}_s\). But \(\mathrm{X}_s\) is geometrically connected (for if \(\overline{\mathrm{T}}\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) and N its normalizer in \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\), then \(\mathrm{X}_{\overline{s}}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}/\mathrm{N}\)), and the image of \(\mathrm{T}_s\) under \(f\) contains the unit section of \(\mathrm{G}_s\); it then follows from Exp. VIB 7.4 that there exist an integer \(\mathrm{N} > 0\) and a certain S-morphism \(f^{\mathrm{N}} : \mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{N}} \to \mathrm{G}\) (where \(\mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{N}} = \mathrm{T} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \cdots \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{T}\) (N factors) and \(f^{\mathrm{N}}\) depends only on \(f\) and on the multiplication law of G) such that \((f^{\mathrm{N}})_s\) is surjective.

Consider the base change \(\mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{S}\) and the restrictions of \(u_{\mathrm{X}}\) and \(v_{\mathrm{X}}\) to the subtorus T of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{X}}\). By hypothesis, we have \(u_{\mathrm{X}_s} = v_{\mathrm{X}_s}\) and \(u_{\mathrm{X}_s} : \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{X}_s} \to \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{X}_s}\) is a central homomorphism. It then follows from Exp. IX 5.1 that \(u_{\mathrm{X}}|_{\mathrm{T}} = v_{\mathrm{X}}|_{\mathrm{T}}\). By making the definition of \(f^{\mathrm{N}}\) explicit and taking into account the fact that \(u\) and \(v\) are homomorphisms, one immediately deduces that \(u f^{\mathrm{N}} = v f^{\mathrm{N}}\). But the \(\kappa(s)\)-morphism \(f_s^{\mathrm{N}}\) is surjective and \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is smooth, hence reduced; consequently \(f_s^{\mathrm{N}}\) is generically flat. Since T is smooth over S, hence flat, there exists a nonempty open set V of T such that \(f^{\mathrm{N}}|_{\mathrm{V}}\) is flat (EGA IV 11.3.10). The image of V is an open set W of G (EGA IV 11.3.1) and \(f^{\mathrm{N}} : \mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{W}\) is faithfully flat, of finite presentation, hence covering for the fpqc topology. The equality \(u f^{\mathrm{N}} = v f^{\mathrm{N}}\) then implies \(u|_{\mathrm{W}} = v|_{\mathrm{W}}\). Since W is schematically dense in G (EGA IV 11.10.10) and H is separated over S (Exp. VIA 0.3), one immediately deduces that \(u = v\).
